figures.gloss: King of the Olympians; sky, thunder, law and kingship.
figures.facet: The Indo-European sky-father office made political: Zeus's thunderbolt underwrites not just the weather but the entire apparatus of law, oath and kingship.
figures.provenance: Hesiod's Theogony and Homer's Iliad/Odyssey; inherited directly from the Mycenaean di-we (Linear B) and ultimately Proto-Indo-European *Dyēus-ph₂tēr.

figures.gloss: Messenger, thief, god of boundaries, roads and luck — and the guide who walks the dead down to Hades.
figures.facet: The god who alone crosses every boundary — Olympus, road, market, and the threshold of death itself — trickery as the very mechanism of communication and exchange.
figures.provenance: Homeric Hymn to Hermes; Homer's Odyssey (psychopomp role); Hesiod.

figures.gloss: Titan who steals fire from heaven for humankind and is chained to a rock to be devoured for it, daily, forever.
figures.facet: The trickster as martyr — theft as the founding act of human civilization.

figures.gloss: Twice-born god of the vine, ecstasy and the dissolving of the self — torn apart and reborn.
figures.facet: Ecstatic dissolution of the ordinary self as religious technology — wine and frenzy as a route to divine union that bypasses civic and rational order.
figures.provenance: Euripides's Bacchae; Homeric Hymn to Dionysus; the Orphic and Eleusinian mystery traditions that grew up around his cult.

figures.gloss: Carried into the underworld, returning each spring; her descent and return is the turning of the year.
figures.facet: The most literal seasonal-mechanism deity in the Greek pantheon — her annual passage between two households of marriage IS the year's turning, not merely its symbol.
figures.provenance: Homeric Hymn to Demeter; Hesiod's Theogony.

figures.gloss: The ferryman who carries the dead across the Styx — for a coin, and only for the buried.
figures.facet: Death monetized: the obol under the tongue turns the crossing into a transaction, making proper burial (and the fee it buys) the precondition of rest.
figures.provenance: Aristophanes's Frogs; classical burial custom of placing a coin with the dead, attested archaeologically; later systematized in Virgil's Aeneid VI (Roman reception).

figures.gloss: The unseen one — lord of the dead and of the wealth that lies beneath the earth.
figures.facet: Rules the dead but is not death's cause nor a tempter of the living — among the most morally neutral 'infernal' gods of the ancient world, feared for finality rather than malice.
figures.provenance: Hesiod's Theogony; Homer's Iliad; Homeric Hymn to Demeter.

figures.gloss: Lamed smith of Olympus who forges the gods' weapons — and shapes the first woman out of clay.
figures.facet: The only Olympian marked by physical disability, and the only one whose divinity is expressed through craft rather than force — mastery of fire that compensates for, rather than requires, bodily perfection.
figures.provenance: Homer's Iliad (his forge, his lameness, the net trapping Ares and Aphrodite in the Odyssey); Hesiod's Theogony.

figures.gloss: The sun who sees and hears all things, driving his fiery chariot from edge to edge of the sky.
figures.facet: Pre-Olympian sun-titan whose all-seeing gaze makes him myth's default witness — invoked wherever an oath needs an incorruptible overseer no mortal can deceive.
figures.provenance: Homeric Hymn to Helios; Hesiod's Theogony; Homer's Odyssey (witness to Aphrodite's affair).

figures.gloss: The Earth herself — first mother, from whom sky, sea and the elder gods are born.
figures.facet: Primordial and self-generating — the only major Greek deity born of no one, from whom the entire divine genealogy, including her own eventual overthrowers, descends.
figures.provenance: Hesiod's Theogony, the primary source for her cosmogonic role.

figures.gloss: Goddess of grain and harvest, whose grief for stolen Persephone is the world's first winter.
figures.facet: Grief as agricultural fact: the world's first winter is not a metaphor for her mourning but its direct, literal consequence — the archetypal image of fertility withheld in protest.
figures.provenance: Homeric Hymn to Demeter; Hesiod's Theogony.

figures.gloss: Grey-eyed goddess of wisdom, craft and strategic war — sprung full-armed from the head of Zeus; giver of the olive, the loom, the bridle and the laws of the city she names.
figures.facet: War as foresight and craft as civilisation: the mind's victory, not the storm's — the weaver-goddess every loom-giver across the world is measured against.

figures.gloss: Virgin huntress of the wild — Pótnia Thērôn, 'Mistress of Animals', who runs with the deer she also looses the arrow at; lady of the moon, of the threshold of birth, and of all that is not tamed.
figures.facet: The wild kept wild: a goddess who guards the beasts and the unwed, fierce against any who trespass on either. Homer's oldest epithet for her is the Mistress of Animals herself.

figures.gloss: God of prophecy, music, archery, plague and healing; son of Zeus and Leto, twin of Artemis. He slays the serpent Python at Delphi to found his oracle, where the Pythia's trance-utterances organized Greek civic and religious life for a thousand years.
figures.facet: The clearest Greek Chaoskampf after Zeus's own: a god's authority over an oracle is founded on killing the chthonic serpent that guarded the site before him — order literalized as conquest of a place, not just a cosmic principle.
figures.provenance: Homeric Hymn to Apollo; Hesiod; the extensive epigraphic and literary record of the Delphic oracle.

figures.gloss: Goddess of sexual love and beauty. Hesiod derives her from the sea-foam gathered around Ouranos's severed genitals; Homer instead makes her daughter of Zeus and Dione. Her major cult centers (Cyprus, Cythera) point toward the sea-lanes from the Levant.
figures.facet: Two incompatible origin myths sit side by side in the earliest sources without ever being reconciled — a sign that 'Aphrodite' names a cult already old and already foreign by the time Greek poets tried to explain her.
figures.provenance: Hesiod's Theogony; Homer's Iliad and the Homeric Hymn to Aphrodite; Herodotus (Histories I.105) on the Cyprus/Ascalon temple lineage.

figures.gloss: God of the sea, earthquakes and horses; brother of Zeus and Hades, allotted the sea in their division of the cosmos after the fall of the Titans. Called the 'Earth-Shaker', he is second in power to Zeus and the object of some of the oldest cult in Greece.
figures.facet: His domain-lottery with Zeus and Hades preserves, in miniature, the memory of a shared sovereignty predating Zeus's later supremacy — the myth's own way of admitting the Olympian hierarchy was not always so settled.
figures.provenance: Hesiod's Theogony; Homer's Iliad XV (the three brothers' division of the world).

figures.gloss: Zeus's sister and wife, queen of Olympus and goddess of legitimate marriage; her cult at Argos and Samos is among the oldest and most continuously attested in the Greek world, predating many Panhellenic sanctuaries.
figures.facet: Her mythic role as the jealously wronged wife obscures an older reality visible in cult: at Argos and Samos she is worshipped as a sovereign in her own right, not merely as an appendage of Zeus's marriage bed.
figures.provenance: Homer's Iliad; Hesiod's Theogony; archaeological record of the Argive Heraion and the Samian Heraion, among the earliest large Greek temple sites.

figures.gloss: Youngest Titan, who castrates his father Ouranos at Gaia's urging and rules the cosmos in his place — swallowing each of his own children by Rhea for fear of a foretold overthrow, until Zeus, hidden and grown, forces him to disgorge them and leads the Titanomachy against him.
figures.facet: His reign is remembered by Hesiod and later poets as the Golden Age of unlabored plenty even though the myth's own action is filicidal terror — the succession-myth's middle generation, bridging Ouranos's primal violence to Zeus's settled order.
figures.provenance: Hesiod's Theogony and Works and Days (the Golden Age); the Orphic theogonic tradition.

figures.gloss: The primal Sky, son and consort of Gaia, with whom he fathers the Titans, Cyclopes and Hundred-Handers, keeping them pressed within her until Cronus castrates him with the adamant sickle and ends his reign.
figures.facet: The Sky-Father who begins the Greek succession myth is defined almost entirely by his overthrow — Ouranos survives in tradition mainly as the violence his son does to him, the founding trauma the rest of the theogony works to answer.
figures.provenance: Hesiod's Theogony, the primary and near-only surviving source for his myth.

figures.gloss: Gaia's last-born child, a monstrous hundred-headed, serpent-legged storm-giant fathered on her by Tartarus specifically to avenge the Titans; he nearly overthrows Zeus in single combat before being blasted by the thunderbolt and buried beneath Mount Etna.
figures.facet: The one figure who comes closest to actually ending Zeus's reign — his defeat is not a foregone mythic formality but the Olympian order's real near-miss, the threat every later Chaoskampf story gets measured against.
figures.provenance: Hesiod's Theogony; Apollodorus's Bibliotheca; Pindar (Pythian I) on his imprisonment under Etna.

figures.gloss: Son of Zeus and the mortal Alcmene; performs his Twelve Labors as penance imposed by Eurystheus, purging Greece of monstrous threats (the Nemean Lion, the Hydra, the Erymanthian Boar) before his death and apotheosis to Olympus.
figures.facet: The one mortal-born hero whose accumulated violence against monsters is rewarded with actual godhood — labor as the mechanism of apotheosis, civilization-making read literally as body count.
figures.provenance: Apollodorus's Bibliotheca (the fullest connected account of the Labors); Sophocles's Trachiniae and Euripides's Heracles for his death and madness; widespread cult attestation across the Greek and Punic Mediterranean.

figures.gloss: Torch-bearing goddess of the crossroads, the restless dead, and sorcery, honored with 'suppers' of food left at the triple way each month; she alone among the Olympian generation retains prerogatives over earth, sea and sky from Zeus himself, per Hesiod.
figures.facet: Hesiod goes out of his way to insist she was never stripped of her older, wider powers when the Olympians took over — Hecate is the succession myth's one loose thread, an elder goddess whose authority the new order chose not to touch.
figures.provenance: Hesiod's Theogony (her extended, unusually laudatory entry); the Homeric Hymn to Demeter (Hecate as witness to Persephone's abduction); Greek Magical Papyri.

figures.gloss: Clotho, Lachesis and Atropos — the three spinners who allot, measure and cut each life's thread; even Zeus, in most tellings, cannot override what they have set, though later poets occasionally make him their father or their equal.
figures.facet: The Fates are the clearest statement in Greek religion that a power exists which even the king of the gods must answer to — cosmic order here is not Zeus's decree but a prior constraint on it.
figures.provenance: Hesiod's Theogony; Homer's Iliad (Fate as a force even Zeus laments he cannot set aside for his son Sarpedon).

figures.gloss: The Thracian-born singer whose music charms beasts, trees and even the lords of the dead; he descends to Hades to reclaim his wife Eurydice, wins her provisional release, and loses her a second time by looking back before reaching daylight. Eponymous founder of the Orphic mystery tradition and its theogonic poetry.
figures.facet: The one Greek katabasis that fails: unlike Heracles or Theseus, Orpheus's underworld journey does not end in triumph, making him the tradition's central figure for art's power and art's limit in the same story.
figures.provenance: Plato's Symposium (the earliest surviving version of the myth); Ovid's Metamorphoses X-XI (the fullest telling); Orphic Hymns and gold funerary tablets attributed to his mystery tradition.

figures.gloss: God of the raw frenzy and bloodlust of battle, son of Zeus and Hera; disliked by the other Olympians (Zeus calls him the most hateful of his children in the Iliad) and consistently bested in myth by Athena, the goddess of war's disciplined, strategic face.
figures.facet: Greek religion splits war into two gods rather than one, and keeps the more disorderly half structurally subordinate — Ares embodies battle as chaos to be managed, not glorified, against Athena's battle as craft.
figures.provenance: Homer's Iliad (his wounding and humiliation, Zeus's rebuke); the Homeric Hymn to Ares.

figures.gloss: Eldest child of Cronus and Rhea, virgin goddess of the hearth; she yields her Olympian seat (in some tellings, to Dionysus) yet every Greek city's civic hearth-fire and every household's domestic altar is hers, tended first and last in any sacrifice.
figures.facet: The quietest of the twelve Olympians in myth is the one whose cult was the most universally, unavoidably present — every hearth in Greece was a shrine to her whether anyone called it one or not.
figures.provenance: Hesiod's Theogony; the Homeric Hymn to Aphrodite (her vow of perpetual virginity); Pausanias on civic prytaneion hearths.

figures.gloss: Goat-legged god of shepherds, flocks, wild places and the noon hour's sudden, causeless fear that bears his name; son of Hermes in most accounts, worshipped especially in Arcadia's rustic backcountry rather than the great civic sanctuaries.
figures.facet: He is the one major Greek god whose domain is explicitly the uncivilized margin — Pan belongs to the Arcadian wild the polis defines itself against, not to Olympus or the city center.
figures.provenance: The Homeric Hymn to Pan; Herodotus (Histories VI.105, the runner Pheidippides's vision of Pan); Theocritus's pastoral Idylls.

figures.gloss: Titaness who personifies divine Law, right custom and proper order; second consort of Zeus in Hesiod, she holds the Delphic oracle before Apollo and mothers the Horai (Seasons) and the Moirai (Fates) upon him.
figures.facet: She is Greek religion's most explicit personification of the idea that runs quietly under the whole pantheon: an order prior to and binding on the gods themselves, of which Zeus is the enforcer, not the author.
figures.provenance: Hesiod's Theogony; Aeschylus's Prometheus Bound (Themis/Gaia as source of prophetic knowledge); Pausanias on the Delphic oracle's succession of owners.

figures.gloss: In Hesiod's Theogony, one of the first four powers to come into being at the very start of the cosmos — alongside Chaos, Gaia and Tartarus — 'who loosens the limbs and overcomes the mind and wise counsel of all gods and all men'; later tradition makes him instead the young son of Aphrodite.
figures.facet: The earliest surviving Greek cosmogony treats desire as a structural force of creation itself, prior to and independent of any love-goddess — a primordial Eros the later, more familiar cherub-Cupid almost entirely displaces from memory.
figures.provenance: Hesiod's Theogony (the primordial Eros); later reworking in Hellenistic poetry (Apollonius of Rhodes) and Roman Cupid tradition, where he becomes Aphrodite's child.

figures.gloss: Son of Zeus and the mortal Danaë; beheads the Gorgon Medusa using Athena's polished bronze shield as a mirror to avoid her petrifying gaze, then uses the severed head to turn the sea-monster Cetus to stone and rescue Andromeda.
figures.facet: His victory depends entirely on refusing to look directly at the monster — Chaoskampf here is won by technology and misdirection, not superior force, making Perseus the craft-hero's version of the dragon-slayer.
figures.provenance: Apollodorus's Bibliotheca (the fullest connected narrative); Ovid's Metamorphoses IV-V; Pindar's Pythian XII.

figures.gloss: One of the three Gorgon sisters, the only mortal one; a monstrous woman with living snakes for hair whose gaze turns any onlooker to stone. Later tradition (Ovid) makes her a once-beautiful woman transformed as punishment after being assaulted in Athena's temple.
figures.facet: Her story shifts across the surviving sources from monster-simply-to-be-slain to victim-then-punished — the corpus's clearest example of a Greek 'monster' whose moral status is rewritten centuries after her myth was fixed.
figures.provenance: Hesiod's Theogony; Apollodorus's Bibliotheca; Ovid's Metamorphoses IV (the assault-and-transformation version).

edges.note: Zeus < *Dyēus by regular sound-law — the same word, three thousand years apart.
Siblings, not borrowings: both descend straight from *Dyēus-ph₂tēr.
Herodotus flatly identified them; the dying-and-rising vine-god read as the Egyptian risen lord.
Identified across the Hellenistic world as the one great grieving mother-goddess.
Fused into a single deity, Hermanubis, in Greco-Roman Egypt — two guides of the dead become one.
Descent of a goddess into death and her cyclic return — the season written as a journey below.
Messenger of the crossroads who alone can carry words between human and divine.
The Earth as primal mother, pressed beneath the Sky until the two are forced apart.
Pótnia Thērôn and Paśupati — the Mistress and the Lord of Animals, the same archaic office of the one who owns the wild and its beasts, split into a goddess and a god.
Two fire-and-boundary tricksters, mirror-opposite: Prometheus the punished benefactor, Loki the gift that curdles into catastrophe.

A reach: both stage a ritual death and rebirth into secret knowledge guarded from the uninitiated — the Bora and the Dionysian/Eleusinian mysteries — but one makes a tribesman, the other promises a blessed afterlife. Initiatory death in both, different stakes. Flagged.

Faint: in both, a swallowing/abduction of female figures by a chthonic power becomes the charter of fertility and ritual — the sisters taken into the serpent, Persephone into the underworld — but the Yolŋu myth is about initiation, not seasonal return. Flagged.

Both preside over the wild and its game as mistress/master of animals; Artemis demands the hunter's respect and punishes the impious kill much as the bear-kamuy must be honoured — convergent keepers-of-the-wild across unrelated worlds.

Both bring fire and the civilised arts to humankind, but inverted in licence: Prometheus STEALS fire against the gods' will, while Aeoina is SENT down to instruct — the culture-hero as sanctioned teacher versus stolen transgression.
Earth personified as primal mother and ground of the dead; both are reverenced as the body of the world itself.
The cunning benefactor who takes from heaven and gives to humankind — Prometheus fire, Anansi the tales.
Trickster as patron of speech, story and clever exchange — both make cunning into a civilising art.
GOLD SEAM: The classical Greek psychopomp was explicitly adopted by alchemists as the patron of their art and the very soul of quicksilver.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Creators who bestow the specific technical and spiritual tools necessary for humanity to survive a hostile cosmos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A deity of dangerous, ecstatic energy, closely associated with plant life, music, and a cult of transformative initiation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The primordial Earth personified as a female entity, foundational to all life but capable of terrifying destructive wrath.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Sovereigns of the underworld and masters of mineral wealth, originally morally neutral but widely feared by the living.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A psychopomp who ferries the souls of the deceased across a dark, cosmic body of water to the underworld.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A localized sovereign of the underworld who rules over the dead but is not inherently the origin of all cosmic evil.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are brilliant light-bringers who gifted humanity with independence and fire/art, yet separated them from divine wholeness.

Classical writers (Tertullian, the Suda) explicitly identified Nabataean Dushara with Dionysos — a documented interpretatio graeca that grafted vine, mystery and the dying-rising strand onto the desert mountain-god.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The rebel-hero who disobeys orthodox restriction to deliver the fire of liberation—here, the Violet Flame—directly to humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The fundamental earth mother who births both the celestial gods and the monstrous forces that oppose them.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Culture heroes who bypass or defy the other gods to bleed for humanity and bring them civilization.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Impassive, skeletal lords of the dead who rule over a morally neutral, dreary underworld.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The sovereign of the dead, functionally and structurally separate from the sky-dwelling pantheon.
Same Indo-European etymon *Dyeus; Dievas and Zeus are the same inherited word in different daughter tongues.
Chariot-driving sun of the IE world; the Baltic Saulė keeps the wheel and the daily course while turning the charioteer female.
Earth personified as mother and source; Žemyna and Gaia share the function and, with chthon, the *dhg'hem- earth-root — but the divine figures are parallels, not one name.
Grain-and-ground mothers of the field; the Lithuanian first-fruits to Žemyna echo Demeter's agrarian cult without proven descent.
Aušrinė's root *h2ews- (dawn) is cognate with Greek Eos/Aurora, not with Helios; the seam belongs to the dawn-goddess line — flagged as a reach, true target is the dawn not the sun.
Wealth-keeping lords of the dead below; Velnias, master of cattle and the buried hoard, parallels Hades the rich one — independent underworld sovereigns.
Earth-and-sovereignty as a single female power; but Mari is mountain-and-weather and morally enforcing, where Gaia is the generative ground beneath the gods.
Prometheus steals fire FROM the gods FOR mankind; humankind steals agriculture and the forge FROM Basajaun — the culture-gift reversed, taken from the wild not granted from above.
GOLD SEAM: Explicitly syncretized at Siwa as Zeus Ammon, fusing the Greek sky-father with the Libyan ram-god.
GOLD SEAM: Deeply localized, chthonic mother goddesses whose divinity is literally synonymous with the earth and sheltering stone.

The gshen funerary priest conducts the royal dead across the death-threshold with goods and a ransom for the way — psychopomp function shared with the ferryman, the Bön divergence being the priest as living guide rather than otherworld boatman.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The intellectual liberator who brings the forbidden fire (the Vedas, translated from Sanskrit into vernaculars) directly to humanity, defying the divine mediators.
The thunderbolt-wielding sky-king atop the world-mountain's heaven — Śakra and Zeus as the storm-sovereign archetype, with Buddhism uniquely subordinating him to a higher law.
GOLD SEAM: Both are divine messengers, masters of boundaries, and trickster guides who move freely between the realms of gods and men.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both center on a blazing volcanic underworld re-imagined as an active, bustling forge where divine items are manufactured.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both induce a localized, ecstatic madness that shatters conventional social order and demands total surrender to the spirit.
GOLD SEAM: Both feature the ritual ingestion of wine as the blood of a suffering god, though the Eucharist transfigures ecstatic frenzy into a sober sacrament of communion.
GOLD SEAM: As Christianity displaced paganism, virgin goddesses like Athena were frequently overlaid with Marian devotion, most famously in the conversion of the Parthenon into a church of Mary.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: CAW explicitly adopts the Greek name and mythic resonance, fusing it with 20th-century ecological systems theory.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The alien culture-hero who brings forbidden, radically transformative knowledge to humanity at great personal cost.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A masculine divine figure representing ecstatic natural forces, wilderness, and the subversion of rigid social structures.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Explicitly syncretized in Greco-Roman antiquity, merging the Phrygian mountain sovereign with the foundational Greek earth mother.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Conflated in Hellenistic and Roman worship, bridging the wild mountain-mother with the grieving mother of agriculture.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Syncretized extensively in Anatolia (Artemis of Ephesus), where the Greek virgin huntress absorbed the Phrygian mother's polymorphic, beast-mastering aspects.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Reason functions as the stolen fire—the illuminating, liberating force that elevates humanity and challenges tyrannical authority.
Intra-cultural syncretism: The mystery cult's dismembered infant directly identified with the mainstream Olympian god of wine.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Ariadne frequently functions as the chthonic bride in Bacchic contexts, mirroring and sometimes fully absorbing Persephone's role.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The tablets exist specifically to instruct the initiate on how to bypass or properly address the traditional Greek underworld guardians.
A primordial figure who seizes from the creator before the appointed time and so alters the human lot; a reach, flagged — premature theft against ordered patience.

The mother who both blights and blesses the crop and the body — Māriyammaṉ's heat-and-cooling of disease rhymes with Demeter's power to wither or grant the harvest, the ambivalent giver-and-taker mother across families.

The boundary-marker and night-traveller who guards passage between realms — Aiyaṉār riding the village edge and Hermes of the herm-stones and the roads, the god of the threshold across separate families.
GOLD SEAM: The explicit local manifestation of the pan-Hellenic goddess, anchoring her to the specific site of the Eleusinian revelation.
GOLD SEAM: The specific Eleusinian mystery-cult avatar of the Greek Queen of the Underworld.
GOLD SEAM: Historically syncretized by the Athenians, fusing the Eleusinian cry of joy with the pan-Hellenic god of wine and ecstasy.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Iboga serves as the chemical ferryman, demanding a physical toll to transport the initiate's soul across the river of death.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A loose parallel; both involve consuming a substance of the earth to descend into the realm of the dead and return initiated.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are heroic figures who retrieve a potent, divine technology from the spiritual realm and institutionalize it for humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both act as the psychopomp guiding souls between the waking world and the underworld of the ancestors.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Epicurean dissolution is explicitly designed to philosophically destroy the terror of Hades and its endless punishments.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are hailed as heroes who defied the tyrannical heavens to bring the light of survival and truth to humanity.
Through Greek contact and Roman mediation Tinia was assimilated to Zeus; his thunderbolt iconography is borrowed from Greek Zeus-imagery.
Shared smith/craft archetype — Menrva patronises artisans as Hephaestus does, though she is also a wisdom-goddess.
Aita is a direct hellenised borrowing of Hades — name, throne and consort imported, the wolf-cap added as the native signature.
Phersipnai is Persephone's name rendered into Etruscan — a transparent loan of the maiden-queen of the dead.
Charun shares Charon's name and underworld station but inverts the office — hammer for the ferry-pole; a borrowed name fitted to a native death-demon.
Where Charon takes payment to row the dead across, Charun seals the tomb-door with a mallet — the same name, the opposite gesture toward the threshold.
Culture-heroes who hand humankind the saving arts — Prometheus brings fire and craft, Tages brings the sciences of reading the divine; the benefactor face of the bringer-of-arts.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The navigation of a perilous river to transport souls to the underworld.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The divine messenger and lord of the crossroads who bridges the gap between the sacred and profane.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The perilous guardian of the afterlife path who requires a specific material toll to allow the soul to pass.
Both the chief sky-father wielding the bolt, but from utterly separate families — Ukko shows the sky-father office filled with no *Dyeus word behind it, the joint reached by another lineage entirely.
Divine smiths of supreme skill; yet where Hephaestus forges arms and divine ornament, Ilmarinen first forges the dome of heaven itself — parallel craftsmen, the Finn raised to cosmic architect.
Both are the generative mother from whom the world's order issues; Ilmatar shapes earth and sky from the egg as Gaia brings them forth — parallel great-mothers of creation.
Culture-bringers who endow humankind with the arts of living; Väinämöinen gives song, the kantele, sowing and law where Prometheus gives fire — the benefactor-hero on parallel roads.
River-bounded lands of the dead one crosses by ferry and from which return is barred; Tuonela parallels Hades' realm and Styx, yet without judgement or reward — a colder, plainer underworld.
The daughter of Tuoni who ferries souls over the death-river fills the same office as Charon — the psychopomp-boatman convergently invented at opposite ends of Europe.
The torn-apart god restored to life rhymes with the sparagmos and rebirth of Dionysus, but the Finnish version is shamanic mother-magic, not mystery-cult — a distant structural echo, flagged.
The divine smith who forges the tools of war and civilisation — fire-craft as a god's office.
The earth personified as great-mother — but Sakpata fuses fertile ground with the sovereign of pestilence, earth as dread as well as nurse.
Divine messenger and god of the crossroads, translator between worlds and opener of the way — psychopomp-trickster across families.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The mythic benefactor who steals or invents fire to ensure human survival against the cold.
The smith-joint turned dark: Hephaestus is the lamed but skilled maker of marvels; Yaldabaoth is the maker whose very craftsmanship is the indictment, a demiurge who builds a trap.
Half of Trismegistus IS the Greek Hermes — psychopomp and messenger reread as the sage who guides the soul's ascent through the spheres. Attested Hellenistic identification, not a parallel.
A feminine figure whose loss into the lower world entangles the cosmos and whose return is the world's hope — the dying-rising goddess read as the fall and redemption of Wisdom herself.

Abraxas of the 365 heavens is invoked alongside solar imagery on gnostic gems and in the magical papyri, a god of the year's full circuit — a solar-cosmic ruler by number, not the daily-driving sun of Helios.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The movement directly reclaims the Eleusinian Demeter-Persephone mythos as a core narrative of mother-daughter empowerment and seasonal rebirth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Dionysus is frequently syncretized into the modern Horned God archetype, bridging ancient Greek ecstasy with contemporary Neopagan rituals of wild, untamed masculinity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent a sealed, chthonic prison where defeated elder deities or rebellious spirits are kept out of the mortal order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both figures harness a dangerous, forbidden power to build human civilization.
GOLD SEAM: Sovereign entities who dwell strictly on the urban periphery, exercising absolute, un-domesticated mastery over wild beasts.

The staple harvest sacralised and given a feminine face of plenty — the Three Sisters thanked as living kindred against Demeter the grain-mother of the Mysteries; the cultivated field made holy and motherly in unrelated worlds.

Both answer the question of where solid earth first came from, but by opposite routes — Gaia the primordial Earth simply arising, the muskrat's mud dived up from beneath a prior sea; the earth-diver's contingent, animal-won land set against the self-born Earth, flagged.

The fire-bringer to humankind reached in two unconnected worlds — Māui snatching fire from the mud-hens or Mahuika for people, Prometheus stealing it from the gods; the trickster culture-hero who steals the means of cooking and is punished for it.

GOLD SEAM: Hermes Trismegistus is identified with the Greek Hermes — both are messengers, psychopomps, and gods of boundaries. Hermes is the herald of the gods, the guide of souls, the inventor of the lyre.

GOLD SEAM: Both are masters of the forge — Hephaestus is the Greek god of smiths and fire, while the Hermetic alchemist is the 'smith' who transmutes metals and souls. The joint is instantiated by the transformative fire.

THE flagship transmission seam: the Hurrian-Hittite 'Kingship in Heaven' (Anu–Kumarbi–Storm-God) is the documented Near-Eastern source-type behind Hesiod's Ouranos–Kronos–Zeus succession, the castration-of-the-sky motif carried west.

The Storm-God-vs-serpent combat is widely held to underlie the Greek Zeus–Typhon battle (Typhon localised in Cilicia, on Anatolia's edge) — an attested Near-Eastern-to-Greek transmission of the Chaoskampf.
Both dramatise seasonal sterility through a deity's absence — but Telipinu vanishes in WRATH and is fetched home, where Persephone is abducted below; withdrawal versus descent.
The divine withdrawal that blights the earth: Demeter's grieving retreat blights the grain as Telipinu's wrathful disappearance does — fertility held hostage to a god's mood.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both require literal, physical currency (silver dimes in Hoodoo, obols in Greek myth) to successfully transact with the spirits of the underworld.
GOLD SEAM: The Hurrian myth of Teshub overthrowing his father Kumarbi is the documented historical source for the Greek succession myth of Zeus and Kronos.

GOLD SEAM: Earth as primal mother and ground of the dead — but Ala is also the active source of human morality (nsọ Ala), a law-giving earth where Gaia is the cosmogonic body. The divergence sharpens the shared great-mother joint.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are tutelaries of travelers and boundaries, visually linked by their broad-brimmed traveler's hats (petasus).
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are fierce, independent huntresses who strictly guard the wilderness and its creatures from human overreach.
The withholding mother whose mood is the world's scarcity: Demeter starves the earth in grief, Sedna stops the seals in anger — fertility hostage to a goddess's wound.
Both ferry the dead across water to the cold realm beneath — Anguta carrying souls down to Adlivun as Charon poles them over the Styx.
Both personify the sun's daily course, but the Inuit Sun is a wronged sister fleeing rather than a charioteer riding in glory — the same body read through shame instead of majesty.

GOLD SEAM: Isis was syncretized with Demeter in the Hellenistic world — both are mourning mothers who search for a lost child (Isis for Osiris, Demeter for Persephone) and both are goddesses of grain and fertility.
GOLD SEAM: Isis is called the 'eldest daughter of Kronos' in the aretalogies, identifying her with the earth goddess Gaia — both are primordial mothers who give birth to all things.
GOLD SEAM: Serapis is the Hellenized god of the underworld, identified with Hades — both are bearded, enthroned lords of the dead, often depicted with Cerberus at their feet.
GOLD SEAM: Serapis was also identified with Zeus — his iconography (bearded, enthroned, with a scepter) is borrowed from Zeus, and he is called 'Zeus Serapis' in inscriptions.
GOLD SEAM: Anubis was syncretized with Hermes in the Hellenistic world as Hermanubis — both are psychopomps who guide souls to the underworld.
GOLD SEAM: Osiris was syncretized with Dionysus in the Hellenistic world — both are dying-and-rising gods associated with wine, dismemberment, and resurrection.

Both bring fire and the civilising arts to a helpless humankind — but Ṛṣabha gives freely as a king and then renounces the gift, where Prometheus steals it and is punished. The culture-hero as renouncer, not rebel.

GOLD SEAM: Both are messengers between heaven and earth, psychopomps who guide souls, and figures who never die — Elijah is taken to heaven in a chariot, Hermes is the herald of the gods and guide of the dead. Both are liminal figures who cross boundaries.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Ferocious, pure female spirits of the wild who fiercely guard the high mountain game from disrespectful hunters.

Two dying-and-rising sons of a miraculous conception — Heitsi-Eibib virgin-born of grass or sunlight, Dionysus twice-born — both shape-shifting wonder-workers repeatedly destroyed and renewed. Functional kin across unrelated families, never cognate.
The water that must be crossed to reach the dead — Kalunga's mirror-line against the Styx that Charon ferries. Death as a watery passage across families.

Both bring the arts of civilisation to humankind, but inverted: Prometheus STEALS fire up against heaven's will, while Hwanung is SENT down with heaven's blessing to 'benefit all' — the culture-hero as licensed gift versus stolen transgression.

Both conduct souls across the boundary to the other world; the mudang diverges by making the passage a possessed ritual drama (the ssitgim/jinogwi gut) she performs among the living, not a one-way ferry.
Great-mother of a people, but Ungnyeo diverges hard — an animal transformed by ascetic endurance rather than the primal earth-body, fertility born of patience in the dark.
A speculative reach: both maternal givers of fruitfulness, but Samsin Halmeoni is a folk grandmother of childbirth, not a grain-goddess of the seasons — flagged as a loose parallel.
The benefactor who hands humankind the founding gift of culture — Ptesáŋwiŋ the pipe and the rites, Prometheus the fire; the civilising boon, though hers is freely given where his is stolen.

A holy woman who arrives among a starving people and whose coming restores plenty and institutes a sacred rite — Ptesáŋwiŋ bringing the buffalo and the pipe, Demeter the grain and the Mysteries; a loose great-mother/culture-rite rhyme.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The rebel who bypasses the divine hierarchy and priesthood to bring the fire of liberation directly to the common humans.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both defy supreme cosmic authority to gift humanity with the forbidden fire of intellect, civilization, and self-awareness.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are outcast divine figures who provide humanity with the foundational technologies of metallurgy and the forge.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Historically and epigraphically merged during the Roman period as 'Ataecina Proserpina', linking their dual roles in spring and the underworld.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both deities seamlessly blend the protection of the civic interior (the hearth/city) with formidable martial prowess in warfare.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Subterranean sovereigns commanding the dismal realm where the dead dissolve in darkness.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The benefactor of mankind who secures vital civilizational elements through transgression against the older order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The subversive figure who crosses the boundary of heaven to steal fire for the benefit of humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A tempest god who impregnates a mortal woman to breed a hero capable of executing divine will on earth.
GOLD SEAM: The dying Minoan vegetation god was absorbed into the Greeks' immortal sky-father as 'Cretan Zeus', creating the localized, scandalous myth of a Zeus who dies and has a tomb.
GOLD SEAM: Ecstatic, youthful deities associated with vegetation, shifting states of being, and the sudden, rapturous epiphany.
GOLD SEAM: The chthonic serpent aspect of the Minoan palace goddess survives faintly in Athena's association with the sacred snake of the Acropolis and her aegis.
GOLD SEAM: Both are the sun personified — Helios is the Greek sun god who drives a four-horse chariot, and Sol is his Roman counterpart. The two were often syncretized in the Roman period.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both rule the subterranean realm of the dead and strictly enforce the absolute boundaries of mortality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ecstatic, rebellious deity who champions intoxication, festive excess, and the subversion of austere laws.
GOLD SEAM: The direct Bronze Age ancestor of the classical Greek king of the gods.
GOLD SEAM: The absolute, terrifying dominion over the subterranean depths, a role Poseidon held before the classical mythos split the realm and handed it to Hades.
GOLD SEAM: The indisputable Bronze Age attestation of the wine god, proving he is indigenous to Greek religion rather than a later foreign import.
GOLD SEAM: The Knossos tablets explicitly record offerings to 'a-ta-na po-ti-ni-ja' (Athena Potnia), the direct link to the classical virgin goddess of the citadel.
GOLD SEAM: Classical Artemis inherits the Bronze Age title 'Potnia Theron' (Lady of the Beasts), retaining the ancient sovereign dominion over wild spaces.
GOLD SEAM: The direct evolution of the Bronze Age boundary-enforcer into the classical psychopomp and messenger.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The thunder-wielding sovereign who rules over a chaotic, emotionally volatile pantheon of lesser deities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The preternaturally skilled blacksmith who suffers severe physical trauma (burning vs. lameness) but becomes a central fixture of power.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Figures whose worship requires massive, ecstatic communal drinking and trance, actively subverting the sober state religion.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The devastated mother whose grief over the loss of her children transfigures her into a fearsome, untamed force of nature.
Goddesses who give the loom and the craft of weaving — but Athena is an attested figure absent from this mirror; logged as a reach toward the weaver-as-civiliser pattern, not to a present node.

Culture-bringers who hand humankind a defining art and aid the heroes against hostile powers — Spider Woman's weaving and counsel against the monsters answering Prometheus's fire and craft for mortals.
Both thief, deceiver and culture-bringer at once; a loose parallel of the trickster who also benefits humankind, flagged as a reach across wholly separate families.

Mothers in whom the year's turning is embodied — Changing Woman ageing and renewing with the seasons as Demeter's grief and joy drive winter and spring; the great-mother as the cycle of the year, separately conceived.

Earth-and-fertility mothers from whom the people descend — Changing Woman forms the first clans from her own skin as Gaia brings forth the lineages; the maternal source of humankind in two unrelated worlds.
The sun as a bearer who physically carries the disk across the sky each day — Jóhonaaʼéí on his back, Helios in his chariot — the daily solar crossing as labour, independently imagined.

First beings from whom the people descend, but the Diné pair emerge by ascent through prior worlds rather than being born of the earth; a divergent parallel of the first-ancestor pattern, flagged as a reach.
GOLD SEAM: Explicitly adopts the Greek earth goddess's name and identity, retrofitting her with modern ecological systems theory.
GOLD SEAM: Historically conflated by Greco-Roman authors as Zeus-Ammon, the supreme desert oracle.
GOLD SEAM: Divine hunters who preside over the wild, untamed spaces outside the civilized center of the state.
GOLD SEAM: Roaming gods of the wild frontier, often accompanied by ecstatic followers and beasts, standing in contrast to static temple order.
GOLD SEAM: Radiant solar deities heavily invoked in late antiquity as universal, diplomatic unifiers across disparate cultures.
GOLD SEAM: Deities who command the hidden, terrestrial wealth of the earth, translating immense earthly resources into divine power.
GOLD SEAM: The esoteric, sacrificial manifestation of the pan-Hellenic god of wine, reimagined as the foundational victim of cosmic violence.
Theft of light/fire from a hoarding power for the benefit of humankind — the benefactor-by-larceny pattern. No descent or contact; a structural rhyme across unrelated families.
The provider-mother whose withdrawal takes the staple food with her — salmon-runs as Fog Woman's gift, grain as Demeter's; subsistence bound to a goddess's keeping.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are tricksters, psychopomps, and masters of the crossroads who alone can open the boundary between realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Lords of the forge and masters of the transformative, technological power of iron.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Lovelock explicitly named his scientific hypothesis after the Greek Earth Goddess, syncretizing myth into cybernetic ecology.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A towering, terrifying deity who arrives from the outside, demanding ecstatic initiation, bodily surrender, and the transmission of vital fluids.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The smiling, slick-talking psychopomp of commerce and deception, leading followers through the marketplace of absurdity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The rebellious fire-bringer, distributing locked, proprietary knowledge to the masses via raw, unapologetic duplication.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The agricultural mother goddess whose emotional state and presence directly dictate the success or failure of the harvest.

Both turn on the violent parting of a Sky-and-Earth couple by their children — but the Māori myth puts the deed in a SON'S thrusting legs (Tāne) where the Greek hands it to Kronos's sickle; the world-parents joint reached by two unrelated routes.

Underworld lords who own a fixed realm of the dead; Maasaw rhymes with Hades as the dread keeper of the boundary, but diverges sharply in being the caretaker of the living surface and the giver of the corn-covenant rather than only the receiver of the dead.

Both give weaving and wise counsel to the people — Spider Grandmother's spun path and craft beside Athena the loom-goddess; Athena is named in the inventory but absent from the built mirror, so logged as a reach toward the weaver-civiliser pattern, not to a present node.

Powers of grain whose generative work runs through the world below — Muyingwa germinating the seed in the lower world, Demeter whose daughter's underworld sojourn turns the growing year; a loose parallel of fertility tied to the realm beneath, flagged as a reach.
GOLD SEAM: Historically syncretized as Zeus Kasios, blending the Levantine lord of the coastal storms with the Hellenic sky-father to protect Mediterranean sailors.
GOLD SEAM: Brilliant, limping divine artisans who forge magical weapons and autonomous technological marvels for the ruling gods.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The patron of boundaries, travelers, and thieves; a cunning rogue who navigates the streets and outwits the rigid laws of society.
GOLD SEAM: Both are solitary figures who journey far to acquire forbidden, transformative fire and knowledge, delivering it to humanity to spark an epochal shift.
Both preside over the wild and its beasts and govern the hunt's right conduct; an honest structural parallel of the wilderness-keeper, no descent or contact between Sámi and Greek.

Both the Sun personified and given the central place in cult and image; a thin parallel, since Beaivi diverges as an often-feminine warmth implored back from the polar night rather than a charioteer crossing a Mediterranean sky — flagged as loose.

A reach: both gather sacred potency into a sacrificial animal whose death the worshipper ritually shares to reach ecstasy — but Dionysus is a god in beast-aspect and the eland a beast through which power flows. Flagged.

Two religions whose core is ecstatic, body-borne dissolution of the self rather than doctrine — the all-night trance circle and the maenad's frenzy both reach the sacred by leaving ordinary consciousness behind.
Loose: the healer who walks out of the body to lead souls and treat with the dead echoes the psychopomp's office, but here the guide is a human in trance, not a god. Flagged.
GOLD SEAM: Both are trickster-messengers of the crossroads who monopolize the pathways between the divine and mortal realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are heroic figures who rebel against arbitrary cosmic tyrants to bring enlightenment, freedom, and forbidden knowledge to humanity.
GOLD SEAM: Both function as symbols of rebellion against tyrannical authority and the bringing of vital knowledge to humanity.
GOLD SEAM: Both represent the unabashed embrace of physical pleasure, carnality, and the casting off of rigid ascetic morality.
GOLD SEAM: Both embody the mastery of cunning, aesthetic manipulation, and navigating the social and material world through deceit and charm.
GOLD SEAM: Explicitly identified and syncretized with Zeus by classical historians in their interpretatio graeca of the steppe pantheon.
GOLD SEAM: Explicitly documented syncretism mapping the classical Greek earth-mother directly onto the Scythian.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Explicitly adopted as a mascot and namesake by Enlightenment freethinkers, symbolizing human defiance against divine tyranny.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The archetypal embodiment of strategic wisdom, intellect, and clarity, championed as a guiding force devoid of martial or divine baggage.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The transgressive, boundary-dissolving lord of intoxication and madness who violently overturns societal norms to reveal a deeper truth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The virgin warrior-goddess born fully armed from the combined power of male deities specifically to protect the cosmic order from chaotic overthrow.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The deliberate use of intoxication, taboo-breaking, and ecstatic sexuality to shatter rigid social structures and experience unmediated divinity.
GOLD SEAM: Roman Sol and Greek Helios were fully syncretized in the late empire; Julian the Apostate writes his theological hymn to 'King Helios' as the exact equivalent of Sol Invictus.
GOLD SEAM: The spirit guide acts as a psychopomp and messenger, safely navigating the medium's consciousness across the boundary between life and death.
GOLD SEAM: Where Hades is a gloomy, shadowy terminus for the dead, the Summerland is a vibrant, sunlit environment of endless learning.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Stoics radically reinterpreted the mythological Greek sky-father into an immanent, philosophical principle of pure reason and fire.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The defiant, radiant figure who disobeys supreme authority to bring the fire of forbidden knowledge to humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Leland's Diana explicitly draws upon and syncretizes the classical Roman/Greek goddess, repurposing her entirely as the subversive Queen of Witches.

Sovereigns of a realm of the dead named for or filled with a fruit of the dead — guava in Coaybay, the pomegranate that binds Persephone to Hades — the underworld marked by what is eaten there, independently imagined.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are entities who deliberately intervened in human evolution to gift humanity with the spark of higher consciousness and intellect.
Great-mother to a sky-father, but Umai diverges sharply — she is the womb/placenta personified, fertility named for the bearing-flesh rather than the earth's body.
Both sovereigns of the realm of the dead; Erlik diverges by also being the cosmic spoiler — judge and adversary in one, where Hades is morally neutral.
Both are the conductor of souls across the boundary; the shaman diverges by making the crossing a living ecstatic journey he returns from, not a one-way ferry.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are cosmic rebels who defied a supreme creator deity to gift forbidden knowledge and self-awareness to humanity.
GOLD SEAM: Blavatsky explicitly conflated Lucifer and Prometheus as identical archetypes of the heroic intellect bringing fire and mind to humanity.
GOLD SEAM: Explicitly historically identified; the Thracian Sabazios became the profound, ecstatic root of the Greek Dionysian mysteries.
GOLD SEAM: Officially syncretized in classical Athens, where Bendis was worshipped as a wilder, Thracian counterpart to the Greek huntress.
GOLD SEAM: The Indo-European sovereign sky-father and wielder of the thunderbolt.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A loose parallel; both are seen as figures who democratized and distributed divine 'fire' or knowledge to humanity.

Both stand as the dread guardian of wild game who turns on the hunter that violates the bounds of the chase — Kurupira fooling him on the trail as Artemis destroys the over-bold; the wilderness defending itself against its own hunters.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The liminal, occasionally deceitful messenger who rules the crossroads, guides souls, and freely traverses the boundaries of the upper and lower worlds.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The alluring, transgressive lord of ecstasy who violently shatters societal norms and rigidity to offer liberation through divine intoxication and dance.
GOLD SEAM: Sovereigns of the dead who command immense respect and fear, ruling an inescapable subterranean domain.
GOLD SEAM: The Maiden aspect of the Goddess strongly parallels the independent, untamed, lunar nature of Artemis.
GOLD SEAM: The Mother aspect, governing the earth, seasons, and fertility, mirrors Demeter's agrarian sovereignty.
GOLD SEAM: Both represent the ecstatic, untamed, and occasionally dangerous vitality of wild nature and male generative power.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A powerful, somewhat adversarial sky figure who brings the vital civilizational gift of fire to humanity.
GOLD SEAM: A divine figure who undertakes an act widely misinterpreted as rebellion, but which ultimately positions him as the active champion and caretaker of the earthly realm.
Both center sacred fire, but Iran's Atar is the kept witness of Truth, not the stolen gift of the culture-hero — fire as worship versus fire as theft.
The primal ground from which the first beings emerge; uHlanga is a marsh-source rather than Earth personified, so the parallel is structural and loose.
Grain-and-growth goddess whose rite secures the harvest, served in processions of women — the green of the field made divine in both.

The defining revival-of seam: the modern Dodekatheon deliberately restores the classical Olympian cult, Zeus at its head, worked from the ancient sources in the gods' own language — a documented reach across a fifteen-century break.
The movement's chief festival is explicitly named for and dedicated to Prometheus — the ancient fire-thief adopted as the emblem of a religion reclaiming what it holds was taken from it.
A young grain/staple-crop deity whose growth cycle structures the agricultural festival calendar of an entire civilization.
Virgin huntress-powers of the untamed wild in two unrelated pantheons — Medeina's forest and Artemis's mountain, independent witnesses to the same religious function.
divine smiths who forge the decisive weapons of the storm/sky-king (Baal's clubs / Zeus's thunderbolts).
hunter deities of the forest and its game, armed with the bow, whose favor a community's provisioning depends on.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: both read through the 'Master/Mistress of Animals' template — a horned or beast-flanked figure defined by iconographic company rather than narrative — though gendered opposite and culturally unconnected.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: a figure who passes into the underworld and returns elevated in status — the retrieval-and-apotheosis pattern the tradition's own gold tablets promise the initiate.
Independent potter-creators who shape humankind by hand from raw clay — Khnum at the wheel, Prometheus per Apollodorus and Ovid molding man from earth and water.
The same god under his Eleusinian cult-name; the euphemism 'Plouton' softened the abductor into the sanctuary's wealth-giving host.

Fufluns Pacha absorbed the Greek Bacchic mysteries under a native name and cult center at Populonia; the Bacchanalia scandal in Italy (186 BCE) engulfed a cult already naturalized as Fufluns before Rome suppressed it.
Turms Aitas takes Hermes's herald-staff and winged sandals wholesale into Etruscan tomb art and mirror imagery — a borrowed messenger-god iconography fitted to a native name.
Direct craft-god counterparts: both are forge-bound smiths shown at the anvil, patrons of metalworking within their pantheon's sky-triad.
A divine craftsman serving gods and rebels alike — but a loose reach: Rokola works wood and canoes where Hephaestus works fire and metal, kin only in the office of 'maker to the gods'.
Cronus castrates his father Ouranos with the adamant sickle at Gaia's urging, ending the primal sky's reign and beginning his own.
Cronus swallows his Olympian children and is overthrown by the youngest, Zeus, in the Titanomachy.

Cronus is the direct mythographic counterpart of Kumarbi in the Hurrian-Hittite succession cycle: both castrate the sky, swallow tokens of power, and are overthrown by their storm-god son — the middle generation the corpus's Kumarbi-to-Zeus seam was missing.
Anu, the primal Sky overthrown and unmanned in the Hurrian-Hittite 'Kingship in Heaven' cycle, is the accepted Near Eastern source-type for Ouranos's castration by Cronus in Hesiod.
Gaia's last-born nearly unseats Zeus in single combat before being blasted by the thunderbolt and buried under Mount Etna.

Typhon (localised at Cilicia, on Anatolia's edge) is widely read as the Greek reflex of the Hittite/Hurrian Storm-God-vs-serpent Chaoskampf embodied in Illuyanka — the node the corpus's existing illuyanka-to-zeus seam pointed toward but lacked.
Phoenician and Punic Melqart was explicitly identified with Heracles across the Mediterranean — the 'Herakles' worshipped at Tyre, Gades and Carthage.
Both preside over the crossroads as the site of ritual offering and passage between worlds — Hecate's monthly triple-way suppers answered by Èṣù's crossroads sacrifices.
Independent crossroads figures of nocturnal power and bargain — Hecate's triple way and the hoodoo crossroads deal share the site but nothing else provable.
Unrelated traditions converge on the same idea: a weave of fate that binds even the highest gods, spun or measured rather than decreed.
The same singer-shaman is Orphism's eponymous founder and revealer of its theogonic poetry — not a parallel figure but the same identity read through the mystery cult he founds.
The same figure placed in his originary Thracian homeland, before his full absorption into the Greek poetic and mystery tradition.
He descends into the underworld to plead before Hades and Persephone for Eurydice's release.
Herodotus (Histories IV.62) explicitly names the Scythians' sword-in-a-mound cult object 'Ares' — an ancient interpretatio graeca, not a modern comparison.

Aphrodite's cult is traced by Herodotus and modern scholarship to Phoenician Astarte, herself a western avatar of Inanna/Ishtar — a documented but indirect line, weakened here to analogy since Astarte, the actual intermediary, is not itself a corpus node.
Direct Bronze Age continuity: classical Poseidon inherits the Linear B po-se-da-o's name and evidently much of his cult standing wholesale.
Sister and wife, queen-consort whose sovereignty over legitimate marriage stands over against Zeus's kingship.
Son of Zeus and Leto; his Delphic oracle speaks with Zeus's sanction as the mouthpiece of the king of the gods' will.
Eldest of Cronus and Rhea's children (first swallowed, last disgorged), granted in recompense the perpetual honor of the first and last offering at every sacrifice and hearth.
Both hold dominion over the untamed wild — she the huntress mistress of its beasts, he the goat-god of its pastures and its sudden, causeless fear.
Second consort of Zeus in Hesiod's Theogony, personifying the divine Law his own rule must answer to; mother by him of the Horai and the Moirai.
In Hesiod's Theogony, Themis mothers the Moirai upon Zeus — Law bearing forth the Fates.
Both personify the impersonal moral-cosmic order the gods themselves must respect — Ma'at as truth and cosmic balance, Themis as divine Law and right custom.
Alongside Chaos, Gaia and Tartarus, Eros is one of the four powers present at the very start of Hesiod's cosmogony — desire as a cosmological force antecedent to Aphrodite's later, narrower domain.
Perseus beheads the Gorgon using Athena's polished shield as a mirror to avoid her petrifying gaze.
Medusa's severed head, given to Athena, becomes the gorgoneion mounted on the goddess's aegis ever after.
The two great monster-purging sons of Zeus by mortal women — the paired panhellenic hero-cycles of the Archaic period.
the fusion is named in the god's own title — Hermanubis literally states its Greek psychopomp parentage.
independently invented primordial mountain/earth mothers from whose body life-giving and healing deities are born.
Classical sources themselves record the wholesale absorption of the Cretan huntress into Artemis — Callimachus and later mythographers treat Britomartis/Diktynna as a name Artemis takes on Crete.
the direct Bronze Age ancestor of the classical goddess, matching the roster's existing pattern for mycenaean-dionysus and mycenaean-hermes.
Fire-wielding divine craftsmen whose deliberate work — forge or star-placing — imposes precise order on raw material, independently conceived.
goddesses of mountain wilderness and the hunt who prize solitude over marriage to the settled gods of the center — Skaði's failed marriage to Njörðr rhymes with Artemis's perpetual virginity.
the same goddess as pan-Hellenic Persephone, given a specific judicial role over the initiated dead in the Orphic afterlife texts.
Greek Helios continues the *seh2ul-/*sh2wen- sun-word by regular sound-law, though Greek shifted the deity's grammatical gender to masculine.

Earth personified as mother and source; Gaia and the reconstructed Earth-Mother share the function and the wider *dʰǵʰem- semantic field (via chthōn), but Gaia's own name is not a proven cognate — a parallel, not a shared word.
at Leptis Magna, Shadrapa was formally equated under Rome with Liber Pater/Dionysus, epigraphically attested in the Inscriptions of Roman Tripolitania.

The medieval source's own equation: the Hypatian Chronicle's interpolated Malalas excerpt names Svarog as Hephaestus outright — a documented interpretatio graeca the revival inherits as literal kinship.

The same 1114 interpolation makes Dazhbog 'Svarog's son' and equates him with Helios, mirroring a Hephaestus-to-Helios ruler succession the chronicle borrowed wholesale from Malalas's Byzantine chronography rather than from native Slavic genealogy.

Both personify winter's death-phase within an annually recurring cycle, but the parallel is loose: Marzanna's effigy is destroyed and rebuilt each year rather than herself descending and returning, unlike Persephone's own journey below and back.
Interpretatio graeca: Vulcan's forge-fire and Hephaestus's smith-craft were equated wholesale, each pantheon's fire-god read as the other's twin.
Interpretatio graeca equation bringing Mercury the god of trade and messages his full Hermes function set, psychopomp role included.

Ceres's Aventine cult was explicitly instituted 'graeco ritu' after the 496 BCE famine, importing Demeter-Persephone-Dionysus worship as Ceres-Libera-Liber by Sibylline prescription — a documented rite-transplant, not mere name equation.
Interpretatio graeca merging Diana with Artemis as huntress-goddess of the wild, sharing the bow, the untamed grove, and the later lunar/triple extension.

Interpretatio graeca merging native Liber Pater with Dionysus-Bacchus; the merged cult's 186 BCE suppression is Rome's best-documented religious crackdown, preserved on the surviving bronze Senatus Consultum de Bacchanalibus.
The Hypatian Codex's Malalas-derived interpolation explicitly equates Svarog with Hephaistos — a medieval chronicler's own act of cross-cultural equation, not a modern comparison.
The same Hypatian Codex passage names Dazhbog, Svarog's son, as the sun and equates him with Helios — the chronicler's own equation, inherited whole.
Julian's hymn is addressed to 'King Helios' as the exact Greek-philosophical equivalent of Sol Invictus, collapsing the two names into one Neoplatonic sun.
Herodotus names the mountain oracle's god as Dionysus outright; the Bessi's oracle is his cult institution in Thracian dress.
Herodotus's own interpretatio Graeca names the Thracian kings' oath-god and ancestor as Hermes.

Two master-of-the-hunt figures who decide the outcome of every kill and are owed ritual return by the hunter — structural analogy between an Ossetian shrine-deity and a Greek Olympian, no descent claimed.

A loose, purely structural reach: both are sovereigns who receive and administer the dead in a fixed otherworld realm, but with no equivalent judicial or agricultural entanglements shared between them — flagged as the weakest of this file's cross-tradition seams.
Structural kinship only, no descent claimed: the chained fire-bringer tormented by a bird of prey, held at analogy given the late (20th-century) recording of the Vainakh tale.
(structural only): an earth-born consort abducted, tested, and finally returned to the earth she came from — no descent claimed between the traditions.

Cognate siblings from *Dyēus ph₂tḗr, not merely both descended through the reconstructed hub: Zeus stayed the vigorous king of Olympus while Vedic Dyaus faded to little more than a name behind Indra — the same inherited Sky Father, opposite trajectories.

Interpretatio graeca in Ptolemaic Egypt equated the ibis-headed scribe of Khemenu with the Greek psychopomp-messenger on shared function (writing, magic, guide of souls) — the base equation later compounded into Hermes Trismegistus.

dossier.essence: The polytheistic religion of the ancient Greek world, from the Mycenaean Bronze Age (Linear B already names Zeus, Poseidon, and Dionysus) through the Archaic and Classical periods to its displacement by Christianity in late antiquity. It centered on a family of anthropomorphic Olympian gods under Zeus, worshipped through sacrifice and festival across hundreds of independent city-states — with no scripture, no creed, no church, and no professional clergy in the later Western sense.
dossier.cosmos: Genealogical rather than doctrinal: from primordial Chaos and Gaia (Earth) came Sky, the Titans, and the Olympians who overthrew them (Hesiod's Theogony); the three brothers divided the cosmos by lot — Zeus the sky, Poseidon the sea, Hades the dead. The gods are deathless and mighty but morally much like humans. Most of the dead go to a dim, shadowy Hades, ferried across the Styx by Charon; the bleak Homeric afterlife was answered, for some, by the mystery cults (Eleusinian, Orphic, Dionysiac) and by philosophers who promised a blessed or reincarnating soul.
dossier.practice: Public religion was civic and communal — animal sacrifice at open-air altars, processions, votive dedication, and great festivals (the Panathenaia, the Olympic and other games, the City Dionysia). Divination (the oracles at Delphi and Dodona, augury, entrail-reading), household cult at Hestia's hearth, personal vows, and the healing shrines of Asclepius filled ordinary life; the Eleusinian Mysteries offered a more personal hope. Piety was defined by participation in the rites, not by belief.
dossier.canon: No sacred book; the authoritative texts are the poets — Homer's 'Iliad' and 'Odyssey,' Hesiod's 'Theogony' and 'Works and Days,' the Homeric Hymns, and the tragedians — alongside the vast record of cult in inscriptions, sanctuaries, and vase-painting. Herodotus held that Homer and Hesiod had 'given the Greeks their gods.'
dossier.reflects: Divinity imagined in the fullest human image — gods who are the world's forces wearing human faces and passions, so that the sacred is approached through story, beauty, and civic festival rather than through doctrine or personal salvation.
sources: Jon D. Mikalson, 'Ancient Greek Religion' (2nd ed. 2010)
